name: Donald Trump
summary: Donald Trump's character and pattern of behavior are marked by a history of discriminatory actions, including countersuing the U.S. government for $100 million over charges that his properties had discriminated against Black applicants and tenants, and implementing harsh policies against asylum seekers and immigrants. He has also demonstrated a tendency to prioritize his own interests over the law, as evidenced by his involvement in over 4,000 lawsuits, liens, and other filings, often related to nonpayment or fraud. Trump's behavior has been characterized by a disregard for ethics and accountability, including his failure to release his tax returns despite promises to do so, and his use of executive orders to advance his own agenda. Additionally, Trump has been found liable for sexual abuse and defamation, and has been convicted of felonies, including falsifying business records.

entries.fact: Donald Trump was born at Jamaica Hospital in the New York City borough of Queens.
Donald Trump enrolled at Fordham University, but transferred to the Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania in his junior year.

Donald Trump graduated from the Wharton School of the University of Pennsylvania with a Bachelor of Science in economics, despite not being listed on Wharton's published academic honors and dean's list.
Donald Trump countersued the U.S. government for $100 million over charges that his properties had discriminated against Black applicants and tenants.
The case was settled with a consent decree agreeing to desegregate, which the Trumps ended up in court for violating four years later.
Roy Cohn introduced political consultant Roger Stone to Trump, who enlisted Stone's services to deal with the federal government.
Donald Trump filed for six business bankruptcies in the 1990s and 2000s.
Trump filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection for six of his businesses, including the Plaza Hotel in Manhattan and the casinos in Atlantic City, New Jersey.
The Plaza Hotel filed for bankruptcy protection, and a reorganization plan was approved a month later, with the banks taking control of the property.
A reorganization plan for the Plaza Hotel was approved, with the banks taking control of the property.
Trump defaulted on over $3 billion of bank loans, and the lenders seized the Plaza Hotel along with most of his other properties.
Donald Trump imposed a travel ban on seven Muslim-majority countries during his first presidency.
Donald Trump expanded the Mexico–United States border wall during his first presidency.
Donald Trump enforced a family separation policy on the border during his first presidency.
Trump had been involved in more than 4,000 lawsuits, liens, and other filings, often filed for nonpayment against him by employees, contractors, real estate brokers, and his own attorneys.
Donald Trump was impeached for abuse of power and obstruction of Congress.
Donald Trump downplayed the severity of the COVID-19 pandemic, contradicted health officials, and signed the CARES Act in response to the pandemic.
Donald Trump attempted to overturn the result of the 2020 presidential election, which he lost to Joe Biden.
Donald Trump was impeached for incitement of insurrection.
The January 6 Capitol attack occurred after Donald Trump's attempts to overturn the 2020 presidential election result.
Donald Trump was found liable in civil cases for sexual abuse and defamation and for business fraud.
Donald Trump was found guilty in 34 counts of falsifying business records, making him the first U.S. president convicted of a felony.
Donald Trump initiated mass layoffs of federal workers after beginning his second presidency.
Donald Trump imposed tariffs on nearly all countries at the highest level since the Great Depression.
Donald Trump signed the One Big Beautiful Bill Act.
Donald Trump's administration targeted political opponents and civil society, persecuted transgender people, and conducted mass deportation of immigrants.
Donald Trump's administration used executive orders extensively, drawing over 550 lawsuits challenging their legality.
Two federal felony indictments against Donald Trump for retention of classified documents and obstruction of the 2020 election were dismissed without prejudice.
Donald Trump was sentenced to a discharge after winning the 2024 presidential election.
Donald Trump authorized a joint U.S./Israeli war with Iran that resulted in the killing of Iran's leader.
Trump opened Harrah's at Trump Plaza, a hotel and casino, with financing and management help from the Holiday Corporation, which was unprofitable.
Trump bought the unopened Atlantic City Hilton Hotel and renamed it Trump's Castle.
Trump paid Holiday $70 million to take sole control of Harrah's at Trump Plaza.
Trump purchased the Eastern Air Lines Shuttle, financing the purchase with $380 million in loans from a syndicate of 22 banks.
The Donald J. Trump Foundation was established as a private foundation.
The Trump Taj Mahal opened, financed with $675 million in junk bonds and completed for $1.1 billion.
Trump filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection for the Trump Taj Mahal.
Trump defaulted on his loans for the Trump Shuttle and ownership passed to the banks.
Trump's hotel and casino businesses in Atlantic City and New York filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection.
Both Trump's Castle and Trump Plaza casinos filed for Chapter 11 bankruptcy protection.
Trump founded Trump Hotels & Casino Resorts (THCR), which assumed ownership of the Trump Plaza.
Trump converted the Mar-a-Lago estate into a private club with an initiation fee and annual dues.
Trump began building and buying golf courses, owning 17 golf courses by 2016.
Trump Hotels & Casino Resorts (THCR) went bankrupt, leaving Trump with 10% ownership.
Trump cofounded Trump University, a company that sold real estate seminars for up to $35,000.
The Trump International Hotel and Tower in Chicago opened.
Trump Hotels & Casino Resorts (THCR) went bankrupt again, and Trump remained chairman until 2009.
Trump University changed its name to the Trump Entrepreneur Initiative after New York State authorities notified the company that its use of 'university' violated state law.
The State of New York filed a $40 million civil suit against Trump University, alleging that the company made false statements and defrauded consumers.
Trump owned 17 golf courses.
Trump agreed to pay a total of $25 million to settle three cases related to Trump University.
The Washington Post reported that the Donald J. Trump Foundation had committed several potential legal and ethical violations, including self-dealing and tax evasion.
The New York attorney general stated that the Donald J. Trump Foundation had violated state law by soliciting donations without submitting to required annual external audits.
Trump's team announced that the Donald J. Trump Foundation would be dissolved.
A review of state and federal court files conducted by USA Today found that Trump and his businesses had been involved in more than 4,000 state and federal legal actions.
The New York attorney general's office filed a civil suit against the Donald J. Trump Foundation, Trump, and his adult children, seeking $2.8 million in restitution and additional penalties.
The Donald J. Trump Foundation ceased operation and disbursed its assets to other charities.
Trump declared the Mar-a-Lago club his primary residence.
A New York state judge ordered Trump to pay $2 million to a group of charities for misusing the foundation's funds, in part to finance his presidential campaign.

The New York Times and ProPublica reported that the Internal Revenue Service was investigating whether Trump had twice written off losses incurred through construction cost overruns and lagging sales of residential units in the Trump International Hotel and Tower in Chicago.
Donald Trump promised to release his tax returns if he ran for office.
Donald Trump promised to release his tax returns if he ran for office, a promise he later failed to keep.
NBC and Univision dropped the Miss Universe pageants in reaction to Trump's comments about Mexican immigrants.
Donald Trump became the Republican front-runner in the 2016 presidential election.
Donald Trump described NATO as 'obsolete' during his 2016 presidential campaign.
Donald Trump was declared the presumptive Republican nominee in the 2016 presidential election.
Donald Trump announced his candidacy for the 2016 presidential election, campaigning as a rich, successful businessman and an outsider without political experience.
Donald Trump made 'explicitly racist and sexist appeals to win over white voters' during his 2016 presidential campaign, according to an analysis in Political Science Quarterly.
Donald Trump's campaign launch speech drew criticism for claiming Mexican immigrants were 'bringing drugs, they're bringing crime, they're rapists'.
NBC fired Donald Trump from Celebrity Apprentice in response to his campaign launch speech.
Portions of Trump's state filings for 1995 were leaked, showing he had declared a loss of $916 million that year, which could have let him avoid taxes for up to 18 years.

Trump dismantled federal regulations on health, labor, the environment, and other areas, including a bill that revoked the Obama-era regulation restricting the sale of firearms to severely mentally ill people.
Trump delayed, suspended, or reversed ninety federal regulations during his first six weeks in office, often after requests by the regulated industries.
Trump expressed a desire to 'let Obamacare fail' and his administration halved the enrollment period and drastically reduced funding for enrollment promotion.
Trump vowed to repeal and replace the Affordable Care Act and scaled back the act's implementation through executive orders.

Trump weakened protections for animals and environmental standards for federal infrastructure projects, and expanded permitted areas for drilling and resource extraction, such as allowing drilling in the Arctic Refuge.
Trump barred organizations that provide abortions or abortion referrals from receiving federal funds.
Donald Trump was inaugurated as President, despite losing the popular vote by nearly 2.9 million votes.
Trump signed an executive order planning for a wall along the U.S. border with Mexico.
Trump signed executive orders authorizing procedures for repealing the Patient Protection and Affordable Care Act and advancing the Keystone XL and Dakota Access Pipeline projects.
Trump withdrew from the Trans-Pacific Partnership negotiations.
An estimated 2.6 million people worldwide protested against Trump in the Women's Marches, the day after his inauguration.
Trump signed the Tax Cuts and Jobs Act of 2017, which reduced tax rates for businesses and individuals and eliminated the penalty associated with the Affordable Care Act's individual mandate.
Trump signed legislation to increase funding for drug treatments in response to the opioid epidemic, but was widely criticized for failing to make a concrete strategy.
The federal budget deficit increased by almost 50 percent under Trump, to nearly $1 trillion.
The COVID-19 recession began, ending the longest economic expansion in American history, which started in 2009.
Donald Trump was involved in the January 6 attack, leading to a disciplinary hearing by SAG-AFTRA.
Donald Trump resigned from SAG-AFTRA to avoid a disciplinary hearing regarding the January 6 attack.
SAG-AFTRA permanently barred Donald Trump, two days after he resigned from the union.
The U.S. Supreme Court allowed Trump's tax returns and other records to be released to the Manhattan district attorney for a criminal investigation.
Trump's administration rolled back key components of the Obama administration's workplace protections against discrimination of LGBTQ people.

Trump's attempted rollback of anti-discrimination protections for transgender patients was halted by a federal judge after a Supreme Court ruling extended employees' civil rights protections to gender identity and sexual orientation.
Trump's administration took an anti-marijuana position, revoking Obama-era policies that provided protections for states that legalized marijuana.

Trump is a long-time advocate of capital punishment, and his administration oversaw the federal government execute 13 prisoners, more than in the previous 56 years combined, ending a 17-year moratorium.
Trump granted 237 requests for clemency during his first term, fewer than all presidents since 1900 with the exception of George H. W. Bush and George W. Bush.

Only 25 of the 237 requests for clemency granted by Trump were vetted by the Justice Department's Office of the Pardon Attorney; the others were granted to people with personal or political connections to him, his family, and his allies, or recommended by celebrities.
Trump granted 73 pardons and commuted 70 sentences in his last full day in office.
The pardons of three military service members convicted of or charged with violent crimes were opposed by military leaders.
Trump described illegal immigration as an 'invasion' of the United States and drastically escalated immigration enforcement.
Trump implemented harsh policies against asylum seekers and deployed nearly 6,000 troops to the U.S.-Mexico border to stop illegal crossings.
Trump reduced the number of refugees admitted to record lows, from an annual limit of 110,000 before he took office to 15,000 in 2021.
Trump increased restrictions on granting permanent residency to immigrants needing public benefits.
Trump vowed to build a wall along the U.S.-Mexico border, and during his first term, the U.S. built 73 miles of wall in areas without barriers and 365 miles to replace older barriers.
Trump's refusal to sign any spending bill unless it allocated funding for the border wall resulted in the longest-ever federal government shutdown, for 35 days from December 2018 to January 2019.
The shutdown ended after Trump agreed to fund the government without any funds for the wall.
Congress passed a funding bill with $1.4 billion for border fencing.
Trump declared a national emergency on the southern border to divert $6.1 billion of funding to the border wall despite congressional disagreement.
Trump signed an executive order that denied entry to citizens from six Muslim-majority countries for four months and from Syria indefinitely.
The Trump administration had a policy of family separation that separated over 4,400 children from migrant parents at the U.S.-Mexico border.
Trump mandated that illegal immigrant families be detained together unless there is a concern of risk for the child.
The Supreme Court allowed the Trump administration's travel ban to go into effect.
The Supreme Court upheld the Trump administration's travel ban.
Trump began a trade war with China by imposing tariffs and other trade barriers.
Talks on North Korean denuclearization broke down, and no agreement was reached.
Trump hosted the signing of the Abraham Accords between Israel and the United Arab Emirates and Bahrain to normalize their foreign relations.
Trump weakened the toughest U.S. sanctions imposed after the 2014 Russian annexation of Crimea.
Trump withdrew the U.S. from the Intermediate-Range Nuclear Forces Treaty, citing alleged Russian noncompliance.
Trump became the first sitting U.S. president to meet a North Korean leader, meeting Kim Jong Un.
Donald Trump dismissed Federal Bureau of Investigation (FBI) director James Comey
There were hundreds of sub-cabinet positions without a nominee in the Trump administration
The FBI launched Crossfire Hurricane, an investigation into possible links between Russia and Donald Trump's 2016 campaign
Three U.S. intelligence agencies jointly stated with 'high confidence' that Russia interfered in the 2016 presidential election to favor Donald Trump
The FBI opened a second investigation into Donald Trump's personal and business dealings with Russia after he fired FBI director James Comey
Journalist E. Jean Carroll accused Trump of raping her in the 1990s and sued him for defamation
Of 706 key positions, 433 had been filled and Donald Trump had no nominee for 264
Mueller's final report found that Russia interfered in 2016 to favor Trump and that Trump and his campaign welcomed and encouraged the effort
Trump pressured Ukrainian president Volodymyr Zelenskyy to investigate Joe and Hunter Biden in an attempt to gain an advantage in the 2020 presidential election
The House voted to impeach Trump for abuse of power and obstruction of Congress
Trump filed lawsuits challenging the results of the presidential election, which were rejected by at least 86 judges in both state and federal courts for having no factual or legal basis
Donald Trump established the White House Coronavirus Task Force
The Senate acquitted Trump of abuse of power and obstruction of Congress
Donald Trump conducted a COVID-19 press briefing with members of the White House Coronavirus Task Force
Donald Trump signed into law the CARES Act, a $2.2 trillion bipartisan economic stimulus bill
Donald Trump halted funding of the World Health Organization
Trump declared victory before the results of the presidential election were known and later baselessly alleged election fraud
The Electoral College formalized Biden's victory in the presidential election
The Republican Party used Trump's false claims about the election to justify imposing new voting restrictions in its favor
Trump held a rally at which he called for the election to be overturned and urged his supporters to 'fight like hell' and 'take back our country' by marching to the Capitol
Trump's supporters formed a mob that broke into the Capitol building, disrupting certification and causing the evacuation of Congress
The House charged Trump with incitement of insurrection for his role in the January 6 Capitol attack
The Senate acquitted Trump of incitement of insurrection
Trump and his supporters began using the term 'big lie' to refer to the 2020 election itself
Carroll sued Trump again for battery and further defamation
Trump was found liable for sexual abuse and defamation and ordered to pay $5 million in one case and $83.3 million in the other
New York filed a civil lawsuit against Trump accusing him of inflating the Trump Organization's value to gain an advantage with lenders and banks
Trump was found liable and ordered to pay nearly $355 million plus interest in the New York civil lawsuit
Trump continued to pressure state legislators to overturn the 2020 election
The National Archives and Records Administration retrieved 15 boxes of documents Trump had taken to Mar-a-Lago after leaving the White House, some of which were classified
Trump's Trump Media & Technology Group launched a pro-Trump social media platform called Truth Social

The U.S. House committee on the January 6 attack recommended criminal charges against Trump for obstructing an official proceeding, inciting or assisting an insurrection, and conspiracy to defraud the United States
FBI agents searched Mar-a-Lago for illegally held documents, including those in breach of the Espionage Act, collecting 11 sets of classified documents, some marked top secret.
Donald Trump announced his candidacy for the 2024 presidential election and created a fundraising account.
Trump's campaign began diverting 10% of donations to his leadership PAC.
Trump's campaign had paid $100 million towards his legal bills by March 2024.
A federal grand jury constituted by Special Counsel Jack Smith indicted Trump on 31 counts of 'willfully retaining national defense information' under the Espionage Act, among other charges.
A grand jury in Fulton County, Georgia, indicted Trump on 13 charges, including racketeering, for his efforts to subvert the 2020 election in the state.
During the campaign, Trump made increasingly violent and authoritarian statements.
Trump said he would weaponize the FBI and the Justice Department against his political opponents.
Trump used harsher and more dehumanizing anti-immigrant rhetoric than during his presidency.
Trump's rhetoric was described by some historians and scholars as authoritarian, fascist, and unlike anything a political candidate has ever said in American history.
Trump was convicted on 34 felony counts of falsifying business records.
Trump was shot in the ear in an assassination attempt at a campaign rally in Butler Township, Pennsylvania.
Trump was targeted but unharmed in another assassination attempt in Florida.
Trump won the election with 312 electoral votes to incumbent vice president Kamala Harris's 226.
Donald Trump's administration asserted a constitutional right to ignore federal law in its justification of several actions, including refusing to enforce a federal ban on TikTok.

Trump's second presidency has been described as having fewer guardrails against conflicts of interest than his first, breaking with decades of ethical norms and raising substantial corruption concerns.
Trump maintained a publicly traded company, Trump Media & Technology Group, and diversified it into financial services while in office.
Trump pursued new overseas real estate deals involving state-affiliated entities and had several branding and licensing deals selling Trump-branded merchandise while in office.
Trump profited from events held at his hotels and golf courses and did not place his assets in a blind trust, as previous presidents had done.
Trump launched, promoted, and personally benefited from two cryptocurrency tokens, $Trump and $Melania.
Trump directly benefited from his family's cryptocurrency company, World Liberty Financial, which engaged in an unprecedented mixing of private enterprise and government policy.
Trump implemented a hiring freeze across the federal government and ordered telework of federal employees to be discontinued within 30 days.
Trump ordered a review of many career civil service positions with the intention of reclassifying them into at-will positions without job protections.
Trump initiated mass job terminations of federal employees, which were described by legal experts as unprecedented or in violation of federal law.

Trump ordered an end to diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) projects in the federal government and rescinded Lyndon B. Johnson's 1965 Executive Order 11246, which had mandated that federal contractors take affirmative action to end racial discrimination.
Trump began his second term upon his inauguration, becoming the oldest to assume the presidency and the first president with a felony conviction.
Trump issued more executive orders than any other U.S. president's first 100 days.
Trump defied judges and the courts in roughly one third of all cases against him, actions which were described by legal experts as unprecedented for any presidential administration.
Trump increased his criticism of the judiciary and called for impeachment of federal judges who ruled against him.
As of mid-January 2026, Trump's orders and actions had been challenged in over 550 lawsuits nationwide, with plaintiffs prevailing in 195 cases and the government prevailing in 109 cases.
Donald Trump was recorded bragging about kissing and groping women without their consent in a 2005 'hot mic' recording.

Trump and Elon Musk's Department of Government Efficiency largely dismantled several federal agencies, including USAID and the Department of Education, unilaterally fired several thousand staff, and reduced administrative functions to statutory minimums.

Trump took a series of actions using the government to target political opponents and civil society, including threatening, signing executive actions, and ordering investigations into his political opponents, critics, and organizations aligned with the Democratic Party.
Trump ended the post-Watergate norm of Justice Department independence, weaponizing it and agencies across the federal government to target his political enemies.

Trump's actions against civil society were described by hundreds of legal experts and political scientists as authoritarian, contributing to democratic backsliding, and negatively impacting the rule of law.
Trump's pardons and grants of clemency favored political allies and loyalists, and disproportionately pardoned 'the powerful, famous, well-connected and wealthy' accused of white-collar crime.

Trump granted clemency to all January 6 rioters convicted or charged on his first day in office, including those who violently attacked police, by pardoning more than 1,500 and commuting the sentences of 14.
Trump ordered agencies to stop enforcement of disfavored rules in an attempt at large-scale deregulation that legal experts described as illegal and contrary to decades of federal law

Trump sought greater government control over private businesses and shifted away from traditional conservative free market orthodoxy, engaging in state capitalism by taking direct government equity stakes in multiple U.S. corporations

Trump canceled and paused federal grants and made large cuts to scientific research, several of which were found by judges and the Government Accountability Office as being illegal and unconstitutional
Trump and his administration's Make America Healthy Again agenda promoted anti-science and anti-vaccine activism, resulting in a resurgence of whooping cough and measles
Trump relied on declaring national emergencies to justify hundreds of actions and bypass congressional approval or regulatory review
Trump declared a national energy emergency, allowing the suspension of environmental regulations, loosening the rules for fossil fuel extraction, and limiting renewable energy projects
Trump withdrew the U.S. from the Paris Agreement on climate change during his second presidency
Trump attributed societal problems to diversity, equity, and inclusion (DEI) initiatives and wokeness, equating diversity with incompetence
Trump reversed pro-diversity policies in the federal government
Trump threatened cultural institutions and sixty universities on DEI and antisemitism grounds, and forced law firms to capitulate to his political agenda
Trump expanded the domestic use of the military and ordered military deployments to several Democratic Party-led cities, and threatened to expand his deployments further
Trump banned transgender people from the military through a series of executive orders and other actions

Trump restricted or defunded gender-affirming healthcare, opposed inclusive language, censored research and education materials, and targeted schools, universities, and cultural institutions accused of promoting what his government calls 'gender ideology'
Trump barred transgender athletes from sports and required U.S. passports to state transgender people's sex assigned at birth
Trump instructed Border Patrol agents to summarily deport illegal immigrants crossing the border and disabled the CBP One app that was being used to schedule border crossings
Trump resumed the Remain in Mexico policy, designated drug cartels as terrorist groups, and ordered construction to be resumed on a border wall.
Trump revoked the parole status of immigrants who entered the U.S. under CBP One and ended humanitarian parole for Cubans, Haitians, Nicaraguans.
The Trump administration had fired more than 30,000 federal employees by late February 2025.

Trump signed the One Big Beautiful Bill Act into law, making permanent the temporary tax cuts of the 2017 Tax Cuts and Jobs Act and adding additional tax deductions totaling around $4.5 trillion, mostly benefiting the highest income brackets
The One Big Beautiful Bill Act increased funding for national defense, deportations, the border wall, and Trump's proposed Golden Dome missile defense system
The One Big Beautiful Bill Act removed tax credits for renewable energy sources such as wind and solar power and for buyers of electric vehicles
The One Big Beautiful Bill Act cut funding for Medicaid and SNAP, added additional work requirements for eligibility, and introduced a $35 co-payment for some Medicaid services

The One Big Beautiful Bill Act was projected by the Congressional Budget Office to increase the budget deficit by $3.4 trillion by 2034, cause 11.8 million people to lose Medicaid coverage, and eliminate SNAP benefits for three million people
Donald Trump posed as a Trump Organization official named 'John Barron' to influence his ranking on the Forbes 400 list of wealthy Americans.
Donald Trump helped bring once-fringe far-right ideas and organizations into the mainstream through his advocacy of birther conspiracy theories.
Donald Trump became the leading proponent of the racist 'birther' conspiracy theory that Barack Obama was not born in the United States.
Donald Trump made false or misleading statements in public remarks, with fact-checkers documenting over 30,573 false or misleading statements during his presidency.
Trump's politics and rhetoric led to the formation of a political movement known as Trumpism, which has been compared to a cult of personality.
A 2005 'hot mic' recording of Donald Trump bragging about kissing and groping women without their consent surfaced, leading to his first public apology.
Trump's comments on the Unite the Right rally implied a moral equivalence between white supremacist demonstrators and counter-protesters, sparking criticism.
Trump reportedly referred to El Salvador, Haiti, Honduras, and African nations as 'shithole countries' during a discussion of immigration legislation, sparking condemnation as racist.
The House of Representatives condemned Trump for racist remarks about four minority Democratic congresswomen.
At least 26 women had publicly accused Donald Trump of sexual misconduct, including rape, kissing without consent, groping, and other forms of harassment.

Federal law-enforcement officials used tear gas and other crowd control tactics to remove a largely peaceful crowd of lawful protesters from Lafayette Square, outside the White House, before Trump posed with a Bible for a photo op at the nearby St. John's Episcopal Church.
Trump's 2024 campaign made extensive use of dehumanizing language and racial stereotypes.
Trump targeted activists, legal immigrants, tourists, and students with visas who expressed criticism of his policies or pro-Palestinian advocacy.
Several American citizens were detained and deported.
Trump's economic policies have been described as protectionist, with him imposing tariffs on most countries, including large tariffs on major trading partners China, Canada, and Mexico.
Trump's second term foreign policy was described as expansionist and imperialist.

Trump's administration favored hard power to achieve its America First foreign policy goals, and dismantled or withdrew support from domestic and international organizations dedicated to advancing American soft power.
Trump threatened the annexation of Greenland, including by military force or the imposition of tariffs on European allies.
Trump selected cabinet members with personal loyalty to him, with the focus on loyalty over subject-matter expertise.
Trump gave Elon Musk's Department of Government Efficiency (DOGE) access to many federal government agencies.
Trump and Vice President Vance berated Ukrainian president Volodymyr Zelenskyy in a highly contentious televised meeting.
Trump used the Alien Enemies Act of 1798 to imprison illegal immigrants without trial at the Terrorism Confinement Center in El Salvador.
Trump ordered the bombing of Iranian nuclear sites.
Trump's plan for a Gaza ceasefire deal between Israel and Hamas was signed, leading to the creation of the Board of Peace.
Trump pardoned former Honduran president Juan Orlando Hernández, who had been extradited to the U.S. in 2022 and sentenced to 45 years in prison for drug trafficking.
The U.S. captured Venezuelan president Nicolás Maduro in a military raid in Venezuela and took him to New York, where he was charged with drug trafficking.
Trump's aggressive and hardline deportation campaign led to large nationwide protests and violent confrontations with migrants and protesters.
Trump authorized joint U.S./Israeli air strikes on Iran.
Trump sent aircraft carriers and other combat ships to the Middle East.
The Supreme Court ruled the tariffs Trump imposed under the International Emergency Economic Powers Act to be illegal.
Donald Trump's net worth was self-reported as minus $900 million.
The Taj Mahal casino, owned by Donald Trump, emerged from bankruptcy.
Donald Trump self-reported his net worth at $10 billion.
Forbes estimated Donald Trump's net worth at $4.5 billion.
Trump's attacks on the media as 'fake news' led to increased searches of the topic during his 2016 election campaign.
Donald Trump had a 15-year friendship with convicted sex offender Jeffrey Epstein.
International approval ratings of U.S. leadership dropped from about 22% to 16% during Trump's first term.
The American Political Science Association ranked Donald Trump the worst president.
Donald Trump's longtime personal physician, Harold Bornstein, said Trump had dictated the contents of a letter about his health released during the 2016 campaign.
Three of Trump's agents seized his medical records in a raid on Bornstein's office.
The first Trump presidency reduced formal press briefings from about one hundred in 2017 to two by 2019.
The Trump presidency revoked the press passes of two White House reporters, which were restored by the courts.
Donald Trump promoted conspiracy theories for his defeat in the 2020 presidential election, characterized as 'the big lie'.
Donald Trump's comment, 'Proud Boys, stand back and stand by', was said to have led to increased recruitment for the pro-Trump group.
Donald Trump's refusal to condemn the white supremacist Proud Boys during a presidential debate was said to have led to increased recruitment for the pro-Trump group.

A nationwide review by ABC News identified at least 54 criminal cases, from August 2015 to April 2020, in which Donald Trump's rhetoric was invoked in direct connection with violence or threats of violence.
Twitter began to tag some of Trump's tweets with fact-checks.
Trump was ranked as the fourth-worst president in C-SPAN's 2021 survey of presidential historians.
Donald Trump's rhetoric was linked to an increased incidence of hate crimes and stochastic terrorism, with researchers arguing that his comments emboldened individuals to commit violent acts.
Twitter banned Trump after the January 6 attack.
Trump launched social media platform Truth Social.
Twitter reinstated Trump's account.
The two-year ban at Meta Platforms lapsed, allowing Trump to return to Facebook and Instagram.
The American Political Science Association again ranked Donald Trump the worst president.
Trump repeatedly voiced support for outlawing political dissent and criticism.
Trump said that reporters should be prosecuted for not divulging confidential sources and media companies should possibly lose their broadcast licenses for unfavorable coverage of him.
Forbes estimated Donald Trump's net worth at $5.1 billion and ranked him the 700th wealthiest person in the world.
The Trump administration did not release files relating to Jeffrey Epstein despite promises to do so during the 2024 campaign.
Some of the files related to Jeffrey Epstein were released, mostly heavily redacted.
Roughly three million additional pages related to Jeffrey Epstein were released, including approximately 38,000 references to Donald Trump.
Trump's Castle and Plaza filed for bankruptcy.
Donald Trump revised the loan for his Plaza Hotel
Donald Trump's Plaza Hotel bankruptcy plan was approved
Donald Trump sold the Plaza Hotel to Saudi and Asian investors
Trump Plaza casino stock began trading on the Big Board.
Three beauty pageants left CBS for NBC.
NBC fired Donald Trump.
Donald Trump released his 92-page financial disclosure report.

sources.name: United States Office of Government Ethics

entries.fact: NBC officially fired Donald Trump from 'Celebrity Apprentice'.
Donald Trump stated that the 14th Amendment is unconstitutional.
Donald Trump emphasized plans to build a 'real' wall at the Mexico border.
Donald Trump was named the 'King of Whoppers' by FactCheck.org due to his frequent misstatements during his campaign.
It was reported that Donald Trump's companies have declared bankruptcy more than four times
Donald Trump's time as an activist investor was reported as lucrative and controversial.
It was reported that Donald Trump retooled his charity to spend other people's money.
The Trump Foundation was ordered to stop fundraising by the New York attorney general's office.
The Trump Foundation was ordered to stop fundraising due to not being properly registered.
A list of 10 Donald Trump business failures was reported.
Donald Trump agreed to pay $25 million in the Trump University settlement.
Donald Trump announced that he would dissolve his charitable foundation.
It was reported that Donald Trump made millions by selling his name.
Donald Trump had gained more than $100 million from Mar-a-Lago.
Hundreds alleged that Donald Trump does not pay his bills
A lawsuit was filed against the Trump Foundation.
The Trump Foundation agreed to dissolve due to a 'shocking pattern of illegality'.
Donald Trump once proposed building a castle on Madison Avenue
It was reported that Trump's real estate projects, including the Grand Hyatt, had financial difficulties.
President Donald Trump was ordered to pay $2 million to a collection of non-profits as part of a civil lawsuit.
An IRS audit of Donald Trump could cost him more than $100 million
PolitiFact named Donald Trump's campaign misstatements as the '2015 Lie of the Year'.
According to The Washington Post, fact checkers found that Donald Trump was wrong in his statements a significant amount of the time.
A poll found that Donald Trump was poised to win the Republican nomination but lose the general election.
Donald Trump broke with recent history by not releasing his tax returns.
Donald Trump secured the Republican presidential nomination with 1,237 delegates.
Donald Trump acknowledged not paying federal income taxes for years.
Donald Trump's tax returns from 1995 showed he claimed a $916 million loss.
Protests spread across the United States after Donald Trump's election.
A historic number of electors defected from their expected votes, with most supposed to vote for Hillary Clinton.
Trump issued an executive order scaling back parts of Obamacare
The Women's March drew 2.6 million participants, surpassing expectations.
Donald Trump issued several executive actions in his first few days in office.
Donald Trump's travel ban caused chaos and protests at US airports
A federal judge temporarily halted Trump's order on immigration and refugees
The Trump administration rolled back an Obama regulation that made it harder for people with mental illnesses to buy guns
Trump signed a new travel ban directive
A Hawaii judge halted Trump's new travel ban before it could go into effect
Donald Trump's potential conflicts of interest were highlighted by BBC News.
Trump stated that he planned to 'let Obamacare fail'
Donald Trump's ghostwriter for his book said that Trump did not actually write the book, despite Trump's claims.
The Trump administration made huge cuts to enrollment programs for Obamacare
Trump signed a health care executive order that allowed small businesses and associations to buy health insurance plans that don't meet Affordable Care Act standards
The United States became one of the only countries in the world not to participate in the Paris climate agreement, after Syria announced it would join
The Trump administration proposed cuts to climate and clean-energy programs
An article in The Guardian reported on how Trump's businesses were booming with lobbyists, donors, and governments.
The United States officially entered a recession in February 2020.
Donald Trump was reported to have the worst jobs record in modern U.S. history.
Deutsche Bank announced that it would no longer do business with Donald Trump.
Donald Trump was banned from future re-admission to SAG-AFTRA.
